ground_truth.title: Melodjinn v0.1
ground_truth.author: Matthieu Le Cauchois
ground_truth.main_content:
This is a technical appendix to the Melodjinn demo in The AI Sound House. I wrote this as working notes, hoping they're useful to whoever pushes the idea further. The code is available on GitHub.

The problem: On the surface, the premise is simple: apply DeepMind's unsupervised Genie framework for building world models to music. After all, music is a kind of world with its own dynamics and actions. But this wasn't the actual starting point. I love music and consume it constantly, but mainstream interfaces aren't built for the kind of daily navigation I want. Spotify's recommendations often feel repetitive, and there's no way to steer them towards what I "need" at any given time. There's a missing middle ground that no tool or instrument quite fills. The closest approximation I found is SoundCloud for its collection of mixes and mobile player, but the underlying axis is fixed: it's the artist's timeline. What I really want is a map: a way to efficiently navigate music with temporal coherence, but along paths aligned with my own tastes and state.

Text-to-music: AI music generation looks like the natural step forward, but two issues stand out. First, most models can't generate in real time, so you lose the continuous thread that makes mixes compelling. Second, musical needs are hard to introspect and formulate in text. The idea behind Melodjinn is generation that can be steered as it unfolds, in directions that feel intuitive to the user and remain stable across sessions.

The architecture: Melodjinn is composed of two models trained jointly: the Latent Action Model (LAM) and the Dynamics model. The LAM learns to discover discrete action tokens that capture transitions in audio. The Dynamics model is an autoregressive transformer that predicts audio in EnCodec token space. At inference, the dynamics model generates autoregressively from a seed, and you can select which action to apply at each step for real-time interactive control.

Toy world: I began with a toy setting using a synthetic drum dataset with a few distinct hits placed randomly. After some painful digging I realized an issue with conditioning the Dynamics model on ground-truth action sequences rather than LAM-derived ones. With adjustments, the toy setup behaved as expected: the LAM discovered a small, stable set of actions, and the Dynamics model reliably reconstructs the audio conditioned on them.

Real music: My first attempt used the Maestro dataset of piano recordings, but the LAM showed no stable or interpretable actions. I then tried Jamendo MaxCaps filtered for techno, still without convincing action structure. I restarted from scratch with zero external dependencies. That rewrite paid off: a 200M-parameter model finally began showing traction on Jamendo techno. The outputs shared structure with the original rather than copying the waveform. However, the LAM learned fine-grained transitions that are technically valid but not the level of abstraction I want for interactive music generation.

Towards v1: Scaling larger models trained longer on broader datasets is an obvious next step. Temporal granularity needs larger patches to push the model to compress information at a coarser scale. The longer-term goal is a hierarchy of actions. Action priors are needed since action sequences are dense. The LAM is sensitive to the space it reconstructs in, and a better target space is likely needed.
with: The LAM learns to discover discrete action tokens that capture transitions in audio.
The Dynamics model is an autoregressive transformer that predicts audio in EnCodec token space.
At inference, the dynamics model generates autoregressively from a seed, and you can select which action to apply at each step for real-time interactive control.
What I interpreted as "noise" was just the model repeating one drum hit instead of NOOP. Obvious in hindsight.
The outputs weren't identical, but they shared structure. The model was following a direction instructed by the actions rather than copying the waveform.
without: Press Start to load models and begin generating

items.caption: Melodjinn architecture. Left: LAM learns latent actions via temporal shift; each action token (colored) captures the transition to the next patch. Right: Dynamics model conditions on these actions to predict EnCodec tokens autoregressively. Positional embeddings and codebook index embeddings are not shown; multiple codebooks per timestep are simplified to a single one.
Early in training. LAM begins to decode the right shape, but NOOP is the only identified action.
Later in training. LAM correctly identifies two different hits and NOOP. Dynamics generates hits at the right time.
